title: Covariance Matrices
learningObjectives: Construct the covariance matrix from a centered data matrix
Read a covariance matrix and interpret diagonal and off-diagonal entries
State the two key properties of covariance matrices: symmetry and positive semi-definiteness
Explain what positive semi-definiteness means geometrically
Compute the covariance matrix using the matrix formula C = (1/n)XᵀX after centering

whyThisLesson.text: In the previous lesson we computed covariance between pairs of dimensions. But when we have 768 dimensions (as in BERT), we have 768×768 = 589,824 pairwise covariances. We cannot reason about these one at a time — we need to package them into a single mathematical object that can be manipulated as a whole. The covariance matrix does this. More importantly, the covariance matrix has deep mathematical structure: it is symmetric and positive semi-definite (PSD), and these properties are what make eigenvector decomposition possible and what guarantee that PCA produces real, ordered, perpendicular components. This lesson builds the covariance matrix from scratch and explains why those structural properties matter.

sections.bridgeFrom: We computed mean, variance, and covariance between individual pairs of dimensions. Now we need to package all pairwise covariances into a single matrix — the covariance matrix — which gives a complete geometric description of the data cloud's shape.

intuition.analogy: The covariance matrix is a complete description of the 'shape' of the data cloud. If the data cloud were a lump of clay, the mean would tell you where the lump is (its centre), and the covariance matrix would tell you everything about the lump's shape: how elongated it is, in which directions it stretches, and whether it is round or flat. A perfectly round (spherical) lump corresponds to an identity covariance matrix. An elongated ellipsoid in the x-direction corresponds to a covariance matrix with a large (1,1) entry and small other entries. A tilted ellipsoid — pointing diagonally — corresponds to large off-diagonal entries in the covariance matrix.
intuition.analogyLimit: The clay lump analogy works in 2D and 3D but breaks for high dimensions. The covariance matrix of a 768-dimensional distribution describes the shape of a 768-dimensional ellipsoid — an object far beyond visual intuition. We are forced to rely on the algebra. Additionally, the covariance matrix only captures SECOND-ORDER structure (means and pairwise correlations). If the true data distribution has higher-order structure (e.g., it is multimodal or has heavy tails), the covariance matrix will miss it entirely.
intuition.text: There is an elegant matrix formula for the covariance matrix. If X is an n×d data matrix (n data points, each of dimension d), and we have already centered the data (subtracted the mean from every row), then the covariance matrix is simply C = (1/n)XᵀX. This formula packages every pairwise covariance and every variance into a single d×d matrix. The (i,j) entry of C is exactly Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ) — the covariance between dimension i and dimension j. The (i,i) diagonal entry is Var(Xᵢ). This compact formula is what allows us to compute the full covariance structure of high-dimensional data efficiently.

toyExample.description: Using the 5 centered data points from Lesson 3, we construct the 2×2 covariance matrix explicitly using the matrix formula and verify each entry matches our previously computed statistics.

steps.action: Write the centered data matrix X (5 rows, 2 columns), using the centered points from Lesson 3: X = [[-1.4,-2], [0.6,1], [-0.4,-1], [1.6,2], [-0.4,0]]
steps.result: X is a 5×2 matrix. Each row is one centered data point. Column 1 is the x-values, column 2 is the y-values.
steps.insight: The matrix form organises all data into a single object. The rows are data points; the columns are features (dimensions). This convention — n rows of data, d columns of dimensions — is standard throughout machine learning.

steps.action: Compute XᵀX. Xᵀ is 2×5. XᵀX = Xᵀ × X produces a 2×2 matrix. Entry (1,1) = sum of squared x-values = 1.96+0.36+0.16+2.56+0.16 = 5.20. Entry (1,2) = Entry (2,1) = sum of (xᵢ×yᵢ) = 2.8+0.6+0.4+3.2+0 = 7.0. Entry (2,2) = sum of squared y-values = 4+1+1+4+0 = 10.
steps.insight: Notice that XᵀX is already symmetric — the (1,2) entry equals the (2,1) entry. This will always be true: (XᵀX)ᵀ = XᵀX. Symmetry is a structural property of the covariance matrix, not a coincidence.

Reading C: the (1,1) entry is 1.04 = Var(X) ✓ (computed in Lesson 3). The (2,2) entry is 2.00 = Var(Y) ✓. The (1,2) entry is 1.40 = Cov(X,Y) ✓. The matrix formula gives exactly the same results as computing each statistic individually — just much more efficiently.

steps.action: Verify positive semi-definiteness: compute vᵀCv for v = [1, 0], v = [0, 1], and v = [1, -1].
steps.insight: For any direction vector v, the quantity vᵀCv is always non-negative. This is what positive semi-definiteness means: the covariance matrix assigns a non-negative 'spread' to every direction in space. This property guarantees that all eigenvalues of C are ≥ 0 — which ensures that PCA components have real, non-negative explained variances.

visual.question: When you make the data cloud perfectly circular (isotropic), what does the covariance matrix look like? When you tilt the ellipse 45°, what entries in the matrix become large?
visual.description: The scatter-covariance visual now includes a 'matrix view' panel that displays the 2×2 covariance matrix as both a table of numbers and a colour-coded heatmap. As you add or move points in the scatter plot, the matrix updates in real time. Off-diagonal entries glow red (positive covariance) or blue (negative covariance). The ellipse of the data cloud is drawn using the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, with axis lengths proportional to square roots of eigenvalues.

technical.text: The covariance matrix C ∈ ℝᵈˣᵈ for a dataset of n centered d-dimensional points encoded in matrix X ∈ ℝⁿˣᵈ is defined as C = (1/n)XᵀX. The key algebraic fact is that any matrix of the form AᵀA (or AᵀA/n) is automatically (1) symmetric and (2) positive semi-definite. Symmetry: (XᵀX)ᵀ = XᵀXᵀᵀ = XᵀX. PSD: for any vector v, vᵀ(XᵀX)v = (Xv)ᵀ(Xv) = ||Xv||² ≥ 0. These two properties together are exactly what guarantees that the covariance matrix has a clean eigendecomposition: C = VΛVᵀ where V has orthogonal columns and Λ has non-negative diagonal entries (eigenvalues). Without symmetry or PSD, eigenvalues could be complex or negative, making the decomposition geometrically uninterpretable. The covariance matrix is thus the mathematical gateway from raw data statistics to eigendecomposition and PCA.

definitions.definition: C = (1/n)XᵀX (after centering X). A d×d symmetric positive semi-definite matrix where Cᵢⱼ = Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ) and Cᵢᵢ = Var(Xᵢ). Provides a complete description of the second-order statistical structure of the data.
A matrix M where Mᵢⱼ = Mⱼᵢ for all i, j — equivalently, Mᵀ = M. For covariance matrices, symmetry holds because Cov(X,Y) = Cov(Y,X).
A symmetric matrix M is PSD if vᵀMv ≥ 0 for ALL vectors v. Equivalently, all eigenvalues of M are ≥ 0. A covariance matrix is always PSD because vᵀCv = (1/n)||Xv||² ≥ 0.

A stricter condition: vᵀMv > 0 for all non-zero v, equivalently all eigenvalues are strictly > 0. A covariance matrix is PD only if there are no directions in which the data has zero variance — i.e., the data does not lie on a lower-dimensional subspace.

A covariance matrix with all off-diagonal entries equal to zero — meaning all pairs of dimensions have zero covariance. This indicates the data cloud is axis-aligned: its principal directions coincide with the coordinate axes.

mathematics.label: Covariance matrix (matrix formula)

symbols.C: the d×d covariance matrix — a square matrix with one row and column for each data dimension
symbols.n: the number of data points (rows of X)
symbols.X: the n×d CENTERED data matrix — each row is a centered data point (original data point minus mean vector)
symbols.Xᵀ: the transpose of X — a d×n matrix
symbols.XᵀX: matrix product — the (i,j) entry equals the dot product of column i and column j of X, which is n times Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ)

mathematics.plainExplanation: To build the covariance matrix: (1) center your data by subtracting the mean from every data point; (2) write the data as a matrix X with data points as rows; (3) compute Xᵀ times X; (4) divide by n. The result is a d×d matrix where each entry encodes the covariance between two dimensions. This is an efficient formula that computes all d² covariances simultaneously.
mathematics.whyNeeded: This compact formula lets us compute the entire covariance structure of a high-dimensional dataset efficiently. For neural activations with d=4096 dimensions, computing all covariances one-by-one would require 4096² = 16.7 million computations; the matrix formula computes all of them in a single matrix product.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without the matrix formula, we would need to compute each covariance Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ) individually — a d² process that becomes computationally infeasible for high-dimensional data. The matrix formula also reveals the algebraic structure (XᵀX form) that proves symmetry and PSD immediately.
mathematics.encourages: Thinking of the covariance matrix as a single geometric object describing the shape of the data cloud, rather than a collection of d² independent statistics.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the covariance matrix captures all relevant structure in the data. It captures second-order statistics (means and pairwise correlations) but ignores higher-order structure such as multimodality, heavy tails, or nonlinear relationships.

mathematics.label: Positive semi-definiteness of covariance matrices

symbols.vᵀCv: the 'quadratic form' of C in direction v — the variance of the data when projected onto v
symbols.Xv: the data matrix X multiplied by v — this gives an n-dimensional vector of projections of all data points onto direction v
symbols.||Xv||²: the sum of squared projections — always non-negative
symbols.≥ 0: the guarantee that variance is always non-negative in any direction

mathematics.plainExplanation: vᵀCv is exactly the variance of the data when projected onto direction v. Since variance is always non-negative (it is an average of squared numbers), vᵀCv ≥ 0 for every direction v. This is the definition of positive semi-definiteness. It has a critical consequence: all eigenvalues of C are non-negative, which means PCA components always explain non-negative amounts of variance.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Positive semi-definiteness guarantees that PCA is well-defined: all principal components have real, non-negative eigenvalues that can be interpreted as explained variances. Without PSD, eigenvalues could be negative or complex, making the geometric interpretation impossible.
mathematics.ifRemoved: If C were not PSD, some principal components would have negative 'explained variance', which is geometrically meaningless. PCA would break down as a variance-maximisation procedure.
mathematics.encourages: Thinking of vᵀCv as the variance of the data in direction v — a geometric interpretation that connects the algebraic formula directly to the visual idea of 'how much does the data spread in this direction?'
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the covariance matrix has full rank (positive definite). If the data lies on a lower-dimensional subspace (e.g., n < d), the covariance matrix will be singular (some eigenvalues will be exactly zero), and PCA will find directions of zero variance.

mathematics.label: Symmetry of the covariance matrix

symbols.Cᵢⱼ: the (i,j) entry of the covariance matrix — the covariance between dimensions i and j
symbols.Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ): covariance between dimension i and dimension j
symbols.Cov(Xⱼ, Xᵢ): covariance between dimension j and dimension i — equal to Cov(Xᵢ, Xⱼ) because the formula is symmetric in X and Y
symbols.Cⱼᵢ: the (j,i) entry — equal to Cᵢⱼ, confirming symmetry

mathematics.plainExplanation: Covariance is symmetric: Cov(X,Y) = Cov(Y,X) because the formula (1/n)Σ(xᵢ-μₓ)(yᵢ-μᵧ) is identical whether you label the first variable X or Y. This means the covariance matrix is the same when you transpose it (flip across the diagonal). Symmetry, combined with PSD, guarantees the existence of a real eigendecomposition with orthogonal eigenvectors.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Symmetry is the algebraic foundation for the spectral theorem, which guarantees that the covariance matrix can be diagonalised by a rotation matrix. Without symmetry, eigenvectors might not be orthogonal, making PCA impossible to define as a rotation of the coordinate system.
mathematics.ifRemoved: If C were not symmetric (which cannot happen for a true covariance matrix but could happen due to numerical errors), the eigendecomposition might produce complex eigenvalues and eigenvectors, making the PCA solution geometrically uninterpretable.
mathematics.encourages: Using the symmetry to halve the computational burden when computing or storing covariance matrices: only the upper (or lower) triangle needs to be stored, since the other half is determined by symmetry.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the covariance matrix is invertible. A symmetric PSD matrix may be singular (have zero eigenvalues), in which case its inverse does not exist. This matters for methods that require the inverse of the covariance matrix (e.g., Mahalanobis distance).

methodComparison.question: How should we compute the covariance matrix for very high-dimensional data (d >> n)?

methods.name: Direct formula C = (1/n)XᵀX
methods.answer: Produces a d×d matrix. If d = 10000, this is a 10000×10000 = 100 million entry matrix. Storage alone requires 800 MB (at 8 bytes per float64). Computing XᵀX requires O(nd²) operations. For d >> n, this is extremely expensive and the matrix will have rank at most n (since you only have n data points), meaning most eigenvalues will be zero.

methods.name: Dual covariance matrix C' = (1/n)XXᵀ
methods.answer: If n < d (more dimensions than data points), compute the n×n matrix (1/n)XXᵀ instead. This has the same non-zero eigenvalues as (1/n)XᵀX, but is n×n instead of d×d. The eigenvectors of XᵀX can be recovered from the eigenvectors of XXᵀ using the relationship Xᵀu = v. This is the basis of 'kernel PCA' and is essential when d >> n.

methodComparison.practicalImplication: In practice, PCA on neural activation data is almost always done via SVD of X directly (rather than explicitly computing XᵀX), because SVD is numerically more stable and can exploit the n < d regime efficiently. The lesson p01-l08 on SVD explains exactly why.

evidence.claim: The covariance matrix of neural network activations is the starting point for understanding the geometric structure of learned representations.
evidence.label: [Course Interpretation]
evidence.paraphrase: When interpretability researchers apply linear methods (PCA, ICA, linear probing) to neural activations, they are implicitly or explicitly exploiting the second-order structure encoded in the covariance matrix of those activations. Understanding the covariance matrix is therefore prerequisite to understanding what these methods find and what they miss.

evidence.claim: Every real symmetric positive semi-definite matrix has an eigendecomposition with real, non-negative eigenvalues and an orthogonal eigenvector matrix.
evidence.label: [Mathematical Result]
evidence.paraphrase: This is the spectral theorem for symmetric matrices. Since covariance matrices are always symmetric and PSD, they always have a clean eigendecomposition C = VΛVᵀ where Λ has real, non-negative diagonal entries. This guarantees that PCA is always well-defined for any centered dataset.

failureModes.name: Forgetting to center the data before computing the covariance matrix
failureModes.mechanism: If the data is not centered, the formula (1/n)XᵀX computes E[XᵢXⱼ] instead of Cov(Xᵢ,Xⱼ) = E[(Xᵢ-μᵢ)(Xⱼ-μⱼ)]. These are the same only when the mean is zero.
failureModes.symptom: The first principal component points toward the mean of the data (not toward the direction of maximum variation), and explains 'variance' that is actually just the offset from the origin.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check that the mean of the centered data is zero (within numerical precision): if ||mean(X)||² > 1e-10, the data has not been properly centered.
failureModes.mitigation: Always subtract the column-wise mean before computing the covariance matrix. sklearn's PCA does this automatically; numpy's np.cov does this by default. But if computing XᵀX manually, you must center first.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: If SetConCA's activation vectors are not zero-mean (e.g., due to bias terms), applying PCA without centering will produce components that partially encode the 'average activation' rather than the variation around the average. Always verify the mean of activation vectors before applying any variance-based analysis.

failureModes.name: Confusing the covariance matrix with the correlation matrix
failureModes.mechanism: The correlation matrix is obtained by standardising the covariance matrix: Corrᵢⱼ = Cᵢⱼ / (σᵢ σⱼ), where σᵢ and σⱼ are the standard deviations of dimensions i and j. The correlation matrix has 1s on the diagonal and values in [-1, 1] off-diagonal. PCA on the covariance matrix finds directions of maximum VARIANCE; PCA on the correlation matrix finds directions of maximum correlation after standardising each dimension to unit variance.
failureModes.symptom: When features have very different variances, PCA on the covariance matrix will be dominated by the high-variance features. PCA on the correlation matrix treats all features equally regardless of their original variance scales.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check whether the variance of each dimension is comparable. If one dimension has variance 1000 and another has variance 0.001, PCA on the covariance matrix will be dominated by the first dimension even if the second dimension carries important signal.
failureModes.mitigation: Standardise features to unit variance before applying PCA if the features are measured in different units or at different scales. If features are in comparable units (e.g., all are neuron activations in the same layer), standardisation may not be appropriate.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: In SetConCA analysis, if some neurons have systematically higher activation variance (perhaps due to weight initialisation or training dynamics), PCA will be biased toward those neurons. Check the variance distribution of neuron activations before applying PCA.

setconcaConnection.relevance: The covariance matrix of SetConCA's pooled set representations is the complete second-order description of how different sets relate to each other in the representation space. Computing and analysing this matrix is the first step of any PCA-based interpretability analysis.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: After collecting pooled representations for a diverse set of inputs, the covariance matrix reveals which dimensions of the representation space covary. If certain semantic categories of sets always activate the same neurons together, those neurons will show high covariance in the matrix.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: The regularisation strategy used during SetConCA training affects the covariance matrix. L2 regularisation encourages small-magnitude activations, which tends to reduce the overall scale of the covariance matrix. Dropout implicitly regularises by forcing different neurons to carry information independently, which can reduce off-diagonal covariances.
setconcaConnection.whichFailureExplained: If the covariance matrix of SetConCA activations has very large off-diagonal entries, the representation is highly entangled: multiple neurons are 'sharing' the same variation. This means PCA will find components that mix many neurons, making interpretation difficult.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Compute the covariance matrix of SetConCA pooled representations at different layers of the set encoder. Use a colour-coded heatmap to display each covariance matrix. Measure the proportion of total variance on the diagonal (trace/total) — higher values indicate more axis-aligned, less entangled representations.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: High off-diagonal covariances in the activation covariance matrix may reflect true statistical dependencies in the training data (certain set types genuinely co-occur) rather than a model failure. Comparing the activation covariance matrix to the 'input covariance' (covariance of set-level features before encoding) can help distinguish model entanglement from data entanglement.

exercises.prompt: Without computing, predict the shape of the covariance matrix for a dataset where all points lie exactly on the line y = x (e.g., [1,1], [2,2], [3,3]). What should the diagonal entries be? What should the off-diagonal entry be?
exercises.reveal: If all points lie on y = x, then x and y are perfectly correlated: every deviation in x equals the same deviation in y. So Var(X) = Var(Y) (same spread in both directions) and Cov(X,Y) = Var(X) (covariance equals variance, because deviations in x and y are always identical). The covariance matrix is [[σ², σ²], [σ², σ²]] for some σ² > 0. This matrix is singular (its determinant is σ⁴ - σ⁴ = 0) because the data is truly 1-dimensional despite being in 2D space.

exercises.prompt: Construct a 3×3 covariance matrix for a 3-dimensional dataset where: (1) each dimension has variance 2, (2) dimensions 1 and 2 have covariance 1.5, (3) dimensions 1 and 3 have covariance -0.5, and (4) dimensions 2 and 3 have covariance 0. Write the full matrix and verify it is symmetric.
exercises.reveal: C = [[2, 1.5, -0.5], [1.5, 2, 0], [-0.5, 0, 2]]. Verify symmetry: C₁₂ = C₂₁ = 1.5 ✓, C₁₃ = C₃₁ = -0.5 ✓, C₂₃ = C₃₂ = 0 ✓. Positive semi-definiteness would require checking that all eigenvalues are ≥ 0, which holds here (though verification requires computing eigenvalues).

exercises.prompt: You compute the covariance matrix of 5 three-dimensional data points and get: C = [[4, 0, 0], [0, 0, 0], [0, 0, 1]]. What does the (2,2) entry of zero tell you about dimension 2? Is this covariance matrix positive definite or only positive semi-definite? What does this imply about the data?
exercises.reveal: The (2,2) entry of zero means Var(X₂) = 0: all 5 data points have exactly the same value in dimension 2. The data has zero spread in that direction — it is 'flat' along axis 2. This means C is positive SEMI-definite (not strictly positive definite) because it has a zero eigenvalue (the eigenvalue corresponding to the [0,1,0] eigenvector is 0). The data effectively lives in a 2D subspace (the XZ plane at whatever fixed Y value), even though it was defined in 3D.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain why the covariance matrix C = (1/n)XᵀX must be positive semi-definite, using only the definition of PSD (vᵀCv ≥ 0 for all v). Then explain what it would mean geometrically if C had a zero eigenvalue.
rubric: Correctly derives vᵀCv = vᵀ(1/n XᵀX)v = (1/n)(Xv)ᵀ(Xv) = (1/n)||Xv||² ≥ 0 — showing it is always non-negative because it is a squared norm
States that a zero eigenvalue means there exists some direction v* such that ||Xv*||² = 0 — meaning all data points projected onto v* give projection 0
Interprets geometrically: a zero eigenvalue means all data points lie in a hyperplane perpendicular to v* — the data is effectively one dimension lower than the ambient space
Connects to practical consequence: PCA will find this direction as a component with zero explained variance, meaning the compression is essentially free in this direction
masteryQuestions.badVersion: State the two properties of a covariance matrix.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Derive the positive semi-definiteness of a covariance matrix from first principles, and explain what a zero eigenvalue means geometrically about the data distribution.

researchWorksheet.claim: The covariance matrix of transformer residual stream activations has low effective rank — most of the variance is concentrated in a small number of directions.
researchWorksheet.requiredAssumptions: That the activation data used to compute the covariance matrix is representative of the model's behavior across diverse inputs; that the model's learned representations are genuinely low-dimensional despite the high-dimensional embedding space.
researchWorksheet.evidenceProvided: Empirical observations that PCA of transformer activations retains 90% of variance with far fewer than the full number of dimensions — consistent with low effective rank, in the evaluated settings of specific models and datasets.
researchWorksheet.strongestAlternativeExplanation: The apparent low effective rank may be an artifact of using a non-diverse corpus for the covariance estimation — a diverse enough corpus might reveal that more directions are needed to capture the full variance structure.
researchWorksheet.missingControl: Computing the effective rank of the covariance matrix for a randomly initialized (untrained) network on the same corpus, to establish what baseline rank structure arises from the architecture alone.
researchWorksheet.closestBaseline: Effective rank of the covariance matrix of random Gaussian activations of the same dimensionality, as a theoretical baseline for an 'uninformative' covariance structure.
researchWorksheet.falsifyingResult: If PCA retained only 20% of variance in the top-k components where k is much smaller than the expected effective rank, this would suggest the representations are much higher-rank than assumed.
researchWorksheet.setconcaImplication: If SetConCA's pooled representations have a low-rank covariance matrix, it suggests the model is using only a small fraction of the available representational space — which could indicate underfitting (not learning diverse enough representations) or successful disentanglement (encoding information compactly).
researchWorksheet.proposedExperiment: Plot the cumulative fraction of variance explained by the top-k PCA components of SetConCA's pooled activations, for k from 1 to d. The shape of this 'scree curve' reveals the effective dimensionality of the learned representations.

bridgeToNext.text: The covariance matrix describes the complete shape of the data cloud, but to USE this information we need to choose how to look at the data. Right now we are looking at it through the lens of the original coordinate axes (the 'neuron basis'). But what if those axes are not the most informative? What if the data has a natural 'tilt' that can be revealed by choosing a different set of reference directions? The next lesson introduces basis vectors and coordinate systems — the key idea that the same data looks completely different depending on which reference directions we choose to express it in.
